content.text:
### Example client-side component with console log in Next.js

Source: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/05-config/01-next-config-js/logging.mdx

This React component demonstrates a client-side interaction that triggers a `console.log` message. When `browserToTerminal` is enabled, this log will appear in the development terminal, along with its source location.

```tsx
'use client'

export default function Home() {
  return (
    <button
      type="button"
      onClick={() => {
        console.log('Hello World')
      }}
    >
      Click me
    </button>
  )
}
```

--------------------------------

### Enable forwarding browser console logs to terminal in Next.js

Source: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/05-config/01-next-config-js/logging.mdx

This configuration forwards all browser console logs (log, info, warn, error) to the terminal during development. It simplifies debugging client-side code by centralizing log output without needing to inspect browser developer tools.

```javascript
module.exports = {
  logging: {
    browserToTerminal: true,
  },
}
```

--------------------------------

### Example terminal output for forwarded browser console log

Source: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/05-config/01-next-config-js/logging.mdx

This snippet shows the expected output in the development terminal when a browser `console.log` is forwarded. It includes the `[browser]` prefix and the source file path and line number, aiding in quick debugging.

```bash
[browser] Hello World (app/page.tsx:8:17)
```

### Options > Browser Console Logs

Source: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/05-config/01-next-config-js/logging.mdx

You can forward browser console logs (such as `console.log`, `console.warn`, `console.error`) to the terminal during development. This is useful for debugging client-side code without needing to check the browser's developer tools. The `browserToTerminal` option accepts values like `'warn'` (forward only warnings and errors by default), `'error'` (forward only errors), `true` (forward all console output), or `false` (disable browser log forwarding).

--------------------------------

### Options > Browser Console Logs > Source Location

Source: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/05-config/01-next-config-js/logging.mdx

When browser log forwarding is enabled, browser logs include source location information (file path and line number) by default. For example, when a `console.log` statement is executed in the browser, it prints to the terminal with the format `[browser] message (file:line:column)`, making it easy to trace where the log originated in your source code.
